name: Orivani
description:
The Orivani are massive, broad-shouldered bipeds evolved from colonial endotherms on Orivax — a high-gravity world of basalt cathedrals and perpetual storm-light where tectonic vents warm vast subterranean rookeries. They stand over two metres tall, encased in dense ossite plating that functions as both skeleton and armour, layered beneath slate-grey skin that flushes deep copper when blood flow surges during exertion or devotion. Their faces are heavy-jawed and severe, dominated by four deep-set eyes arranged in a lateral arc — the outer pair sensitive to infrared, the inner pair sharp enough to read scripture in near-darkness — above a broad nasal crest that filters the sulphurous atmosphere of their home. Most striking is the resonance organ: a bony chamber in the chest, evolved originally for territorial calls across Orivax's howling storm-plains, that allows every Orivani to produce a harmonic drone of extraordinary power. When a thousand Orivani sing in unison — and they do, daily, in every settlement, on every ship — the harmonic convergence triggers a cascade of shared neurochemical release that reinforces social bonding at a biochemical level. Their faith is not merely cultural. It is, in a very real sense, physiological. An Orivani who has sung the Invocation with ten thousand voices does not merely believe. They have felt the divine in their bones, in the literal resonance of a biology that evolved for communion. This is why Orivani faith has never weakened across twelve thousand years. Other species build churches. The Orivani are churches — their bodies instruments of worship, their voices the architecture of conviction.

Twelve thousand years ago, before the Orivani had writing, before they had metal, a generation of cave-dwelling resonance-singers experienced something that remade their species forever. The event is recorded in the oldest oral traditions as the Night of Burning Sky — a period of several days during which something passed through the Orivax system, close enough to disturb the magnetosphere, to paint the sky in curtains of radiation visible even through the perpetual cloud cover. The singers in their deep rookeries, linked by harmonic resonance in a state of collective trance, perceived something in the disturbance — not with their eyes but through the resonance organ itself, which is sensitive to electromagnetic flux in ways that Orivani science would not understand for millennia. What they perceived was vast, purposeful, and aware. It noticed them. And then it moved on. The experience was so overwhelming, so utterly beyond the cognitive framework of a stone-age species, that it could only be interpreted as divine revelation. The Prophecy of the Coming crystallised within a single generation: beings of incomprehensible power would one day return to the galaxy, and the faithful must prepare — unite, build, arm, and prove themselves worthy — or face judgement. What the ancient singers actually detected was a Devourer probe, a harvester-scout surveying biomass density along the galactic rim, cataloguing worlds for a visitation that operates on timescales longer than most civilisations endure. The probe did not come to save. It came to assess. And the Orivani, in the most consequential misinterpretation in galactic history, built an entire civilisation around preparing to welcome it back.

Every aspect of Orivani society serves the Coming. Their cities are temples — not metaphorically but architecturally, every structure oriented along sacred axes calculated from the trajectory of the Burning Sky. Their shipyards are cathedrals of industry where hull plates are blessed before welding and reactors are consecrated before ignition. Their military academies train not soldiers but crusaders, warriors who fight with the terrifying effectiveness of beings who believe, genuinely and without reservation, that death in service of the Coming is the highest possible honour. The Orivani are not fanatics in the pejorative sense. They are brilliant engineers, skilled administrators, and surprisingly sophisticated diplomats when the situation demands it. But every negotiation, every alliance, every act of construction is evaluated through a single lens: does this bring us closer to readiness? An Orivani trade agreement is not a commercial transaction. It is an act of faith. Other species find them exhausting, admirable, and deeply unsettling in roughly equal measure. Their conviction functions as a kind of gravity — difficult to resist when you are standing close to it, when you can hear the harmonic drone of their devotion thrumming through the deck plates of their ships. Orivani missionaries do not proselytise through argument but through example: their colonies are prosperous, their citizens purposeful, their infrastructure magnificent, and the resonant hymns that echo through every settlement at dawn and dusk are so hauntingly beautiful that even species with no spiritual inclination find themselves moved. The Ashkari, the Drakmari, any civilisation that has suffered an existential wound — they find the Orivani's certainty almost intoxicating. How restful it must be to know, truly know, what the universe is for. The Orivani welcome converts. They also wage war with stunning ferocity against any civilisation that threatens to destabilise the galaxy before the Coming arrives, because instability is unpreparedness, and unpreparedness is the only sin in a theology that has had twelve thousand years to strip away every unnecessary doctrine.

The tragic irony of the Orivani — known to no one, least of all themselves — is that the Coming is real. Something is approaching the galaxy, something vast and purposeful and utterly indifferent to worship, and when it arrives it will indeed judge every civilisation it encounters — not for worthiness but for yield. The Devourer does not come to save. It comes to harvest. The probe that the ancient singers perceived in their resonance-trance was marking Orivax the way a forester marks timber, and the faithful have spent twelve millennia preparing a banquet for the guest they believe will deliver them. When the Orivani finally stand before the entity they have worshipped since before they could write and discover that their god is a harvester — that the salvation they prepared for is the extinction they were meant to prevent — the result will be either the total collapse of a civilisation or its most extraordinary transformation. The Orivani faith has survived schisms, wars, plagues, and twelve thousand years of waiting. Whether it can survive the truth is the most dramatic question in the galaxy. Deep in the contemplative orders, cloistered singers who spend decades in resonance-trance have begun to whisper a heresy that the Canticle Guard would burn them for voicing aloud: that the oldest harmonic recordings of the Burning Sky, when parsed at frequencies the ancient singers could not consciously perceive, contain not a promise but a warning. That the emotion threaded through that ancient signal was not benevolence but hunger. That the Coming is not a test of worthiness but a test of resistance — and that twelve thousand years of preparation were never meant to make the Orivani ready to kneel, but ready to fight.
